comments.content:
[IMAGE:0] (Yes, that's "Magic Monday" in Breton. Those are also Breton Druids in the photo.  I'm doing the Brythonic Celtic languages as this month's theme.) 

Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. Thank you, and see you next Monday!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: A few days ago, I was doing my Sphere of Protection, and a stressful conversation was taking place in another room. I couldn't tune it out, so I started humming tunelessly as I was doing the Circulation of Light (humming during the SoP is something I have never done before) to try to drown it out long enough to finish the ritual in semi-peace, which I did. I did not feel great about the SoP, as I ended feeling frazzled and irritated instead of peaceful, cleansed, and protected. I was actually thinking about repeating it in a new location devoid of argumentative family members (I don't do it more than once a day in general, but felt as if it had failed this time), but just as I was stepping out of my usual position facing east, my back grew hot as if someone had placed a heating pad or hot water bottle against it--not a slight warmth, but really very noticeable, almost shocking. I did not repeat the ritual that day. Does the experience of the weird heat suggest anything to you at all?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You managed to trigger an energy release, probably up the spinal channel. It's something that happens now and again in magical practice, and humming -- or certain other kinds of vocalization -- can help set it off. If it begins happening often, you may have a bit of a rough road ahead of you, but now and again it's not something to worry about.

Intrusive noises are one of those things mages in training have to learn to live with. I've live in apartments most of my adult life, and having loud music or a loud argument suddenly start while I'm doing a working or a practice -- well, let's just say been there, done that, used the t-shirt to clean up the mess. With practice, you can learn to filter it out...but it takes a while.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for your reply, and for the opportunity to ask these questions! I wonder if the energy release has anything to do with some other changes I've noticed in the days since--my visualization improved in the SoP, which it has refused to do for an extended period of time (very contrarily, just when I had decided not to worry about it), and, although the process is somewhat volatile, some long-festering issues in the family are getting addressed, and I am handling them in a surprisingly proactive manner. I've also been experiencing a surge in creativity and synthesis between various pursuits. Could be pure coincidence, of course, but "energy release" rather has the ring of blockages getting unblocked--am I at all correct in surmising this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Those could very well all be related, as that's the sort of thing that can happen when the energy channels get unblocked.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting! Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
And the loud-music lovers always listen to something no one would want to hear!

If you jinx the musical moron’s stereo, rather than him, do you still have to worry about blowback?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Every magical action has blowback -- that is to say, in every magical action, the power passes through you to its target, and so it affects you as well as the target. Choose your blowback wisely.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Our furnace just died, at the ripe old age of 22, so right now renting doesn’t look all that bad!

Fortunately we live in a small town where everyone knows who the good contractors are. One of them will be installing the new furnace tomorrow.

comments.title: Central Ray
comments.content: What would be the result if you used the opposite-gendered divine name (i.e. Sul for a man, Coel for a woman) in the Central Ray ritual?

replies.title: Re: Central Ray
replies.content:
You'd run the risk of messing up the energy flows in your etheric body, which shares the pattern of the physical body. It's important here to recognize that the physical body has its own reality; whatever gender a person may identify with in personal and emotional terms, or what have you, the physical body is what it is, and while it's not the whole person it does need to be taken into account.

Work with the divine name of the opposite gender in the ninth sphere takes place in Sphere workings, which will be published in detail in a forthcoming book. In those, you're not moving energies through the subtle channels of your body, you're working with macrocosmic energies, and having the gender polarity to work with is helpful rather than the opposite.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Do magicians go through a dark night of the soul? I've heard different things about it in other traditions. Some regard it as an unavoidable and necessary part of the process. Others think of it like overtraining in an athlete and a better training programme would have prevented it.

On the subject of political magic it seems to me the best thing mages on the left could do would be a working to let the oppressed and exploited see the world as it really is. That would be a very long lever for change, it's non-coercive and the backlash would be potentially very enlightening as well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The difficulty in answering your first question is that the phrase "the dark night of the soul" has multiple meanings. Its original meaning was given to it be Juan de la Cruz in his book of the same name, where it's one of the stages of Christian mysticism, characterized by spiritual "dryness" and a sense of God's absence. From there it's been stretched and lopped to fit any number of Procrustean beds.

The magical traditions that are purely practical in nature, and don't pursue spiritual goals, don't have to deal with anything of the kind. Those that have a theurgic focus, seeking the transformation of the self, very often reference some such process, but it's usually a lot less emotionally wrenching than the state de la Cruz (or, for that matter, Evelyn Underhill) describe. In the Golden Dawn tradition and other magical traditions related to it, it's described as the encounter with the Abyss,which is the ontological gap between the upper three and lower seven spheres of the Tree of Life, and is more cognitive than emotional -- a process by which the individual mage has to open himself or herself to the experience and perspectives of the Higher Genius, the part of the self that exists beyond space, time, and change.

Your political magic idea would be interesting. Me, I'd rather see a working to encourage everyone to see the world as it really is -- it seems to me that if the privileged middle and upper middle classes lost the ability to ignore the impact of their lifestyles and politics on the working classes and the poor, we'd see a lot less nonsense of certain kinds, and maybe even some constructive change.

comments.title: energy gifting and divination
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Thank you for taking the time to host this and answer our questions.

I'm wondering if it is possible or advisable for one person to give energy to another. my wife has too much energy while i have too little and we agree it would be nice swap some. How would this be done? Would prayer work? She is christian and there are many stories of minor (e.g. within the known laws of physics) miracles in her church.

Does asking a god what they think about something directly often disagree with the dogma of the church worshipping them?

what are the etheric effects of sleeping to together over a lifetime?

What is the best place to buy your books so that the largest proportion of the price goes to you as royalties?

Is it possible to have a "conversation" through repeated divination? Or would it be more effective to meditate on each answer for a while.

I hope that's not too many questions, please answer what you have time for.

replies.title: Re: energy gifting and divination
replies.content:
1) Be careful about phrases such as "too much" and "too little." It's not always obvious who needs how much energy! You can certainly approach the question through prayer, as gods know more about these things than human beings do.

2) Depends on the god and the church. I suspect, though, that the more loudly the church proclaims that it has the unalloyed truth, the more likely the god it worships is to have a different opinion.

3) Do you mean literally sleeping together, or do you mean that phrase as a euphemism for having sex? Both have effects, but they're different.

4) Go directly to the publisher's website and buy it from there. Thank you for asking!

5) Depends on the method of divination, but almost always it's best to meditate on one reading, to get the most out of it, before casting another.

replies.title: Re: energy gifting and divination
replies.content:
Thank for taking the time to answer my questions.

With regards to the number 3) I'm thinking of both, can you give a brief overview of both effects. Do you have any good resources on the effects of marriage from an occult perspective that you would recommend?

I think that uses up my questions for the week.

replies.title: Re: energy gifting and divination
replies.content:
I have yet to see a really good book on the magical dimensions of marriage. You either get too much Victorianism or you get too much libertinism.

The very short form is that prolonged physical contact between any two people tends to create resonances in the energy bodies; to sleep together (as in, literally, sleep) is a more intense version of this. The two of you will tend to share energy cycles, to balance one another, to begin adopting elements of each other's tastes and opinions. With longtime couples this approximates telepathy -- have you met the kind of elderly couple who can finish each other's sentences?

Sex produces a much deeper linkage, with implications that very often go beyond a single lifetime. During lovemaking you have a single energy body, and afterwards both bodies retain substantial elements of the others' energies. That has complex magical implications, especially if one or both of you are engaged in spiritual practices.

replies.title: Re: energy gifting and divination
replies.content:
My wife and I have been very happily married for over 50 years. In my own inherited variety of occultism, as a person grows from infancy to old age, he or she develops a considerable number of alternate selves, of which only one is "waking" at a time while the others are quiescent or even completely asleep. (This differs from multiple personality disorder in that the waking and quiescent selves are aware of one another, generally work well together, and trade off with one another according to the needs of the moment.) After so many decades of marriage, we have found that along with our various separate alternate selves we have also developed an alternate self that we share between us. It goes somewhat beyond simply being able to finish one another's sentences. We sometimes discover, for example, that each of us has independently come up with one and the same plan for an activity before we talk about what we might do with our free time on some specific day. This is not a thing that we deliberately made to happen; it just developed naturally between us, and rather surprised both of us once we noticed it.

(By way of a historical footnote, this notion of multiple selves working together in a single person was articulated by Charles G. Leland in several of his works, and before him it had also been developed by some of the earliest Victorian psychical researchers, e.g., Frederic W. H. Myers.)

Robert Mathiesen

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In Mystery Teachings you say it's a bad idea to concentrate and store energy. But in CGD magic energy is accumulated in the inner elemental temples and the serpent's egg. How are these different?

What is the largest scale alchemy has ever been done on, and what is the largest scale it could be done on? The idea of an alchemy works the size of the Orgreave coking plant appeals to a twisted part of me. :)

Since I started reading about magic it's affected my dreams. You know that anxiety dream where you're lost on public transport - I've had the same dream where I'm lost on the Tree of Life. I kept pathworking back and forth but couldn't find where I was supposed to be. :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, in Mystery Teachings I say it's a bad idea to accumulate energy, in the sense of hoarding it and not letting any of it out. Just as it's entirely sensible to stash some money in a bank account to tide you over variations in income, knowing that you'll be putting money in and taking it out regularly, it's sensible to stash some magical energy in certain aetheric structures -- whether on the inner planes or in a magical working tool -- knowing that energy will be moving through it, now building up, now flowing out.

2) Alchemy is a very small-scale process, because it depends on the interaction between the materia and the consciousness of the alchemist. To get any kind of result, the maximum amounts are measured in ounces. Sorry!

3) I used to have pathworking dreams too, but fortunately not that one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG
I can see why number and sacred geometry are interesting (I am getting on much further with Frances Yates just now and trying to spot some of your spoor tracks ahead of me across the pages. Smile.)
My caveat for the following is that my understanding of maths and its amazing history has remained seriously deficient all my life.

You wrote last week to a correspondent on MM: “… In sacred geometry, and the number lore that goes with it, the absence of zero expresses itself in an interesting way. Do you recall the "number line" you got taught in elementary school? It runs like this...
...-3...-2...-1...0...1...2...3...
...and so on to positive and negative infinity in either direction. (What, exactly, is a "negative infinity"?) In sacred geometry, by contrast, we use a number line that centers on 1, not on 0...
...1/4...1/3...1/2...1...2...3...4...” -- ...which goes to zero on one end and infinity on the other [bold added] -and both of these are recognized as wholly imaginary constructs. Neither zero nor infinity are real, and neither one is useful in the proportional mathematics that we use in sacred geometry (and that artists and architects used to use back in the day when art and architecture were a lot less ugly than they are today."

My immediate thought was to question whether ‘your’ series in the proportional mathematics goes to ‘infinitesimal’ rather than ‘zero at one end’. Additionally it has occurred to me after a little bit of reading that ‘zero' might actually be a geometrical property, for example, ‘flat’ within a larger category of ‘slopes’. My further thought is that the concept of infinitesimal is real enough and allows logical proofs about curves and rates (time). And with curves we can see maxima and minima, tangents, slopes, the relations of square and circle; for the latter see Yates again. And eventually Calculus emerges as a tool.

Geometry seems to be about proofs – logical steps in series that one can always come back to. I am interested in recognition, identity, insight and understanding – which are intelligible but not readily explicable. I find it a necessary part of being conscious to make constant reference to my senses, and perhaps thus to what earlier cosmology referred to as macrocosm and microcosm. Question. Change seems the essential of magic, but reference to identity appears equally necessary. I once did some ‘mind experiments’ in imagination, and shrank a thought toward an infinitesimal. It seemed possible for a thought, an identification, to be extinguished, to ‘wink out’ – some kind of minimum.

I have reached the point in Yates when Fludd has a struggle with Kepler. Neither had reached to Calculus but Kepler was scathingly impatient with Fludd. Leibniz and Newton were to come. (Question)If divination, star charts and etc. ‘works’ (your word), we have a way to go yet, or as Yates says of Bruno, there is a determination to find a method for the imagination yet to come.

Hmm … I have a feeling I am going to ‘end up’ with poetry and/or staring at the back of my own head.
best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The number line centered at 1 goes to zero on the one end the same way it goes to infinity on the other, never quite reaching either one -- in terms of the older mathematics, the opposite of infinitesimal is not infinite, but immense -- this used to be a technical term, "too big to measure," before it took on its present metaphorical sense of "huge."

And poetry isn't a bad place to end up...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for this, I have long been fascinated by immense numbers, and having such a handy term for them is pleasing.

Ray Wharton.

comments.title: Giordano Bruno’s magical images
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Thank you for translating Bruno’s book. It’s a beautiful book as art object, and also fascinating. And definitely useful...but After a careful read of The Shadows of the Ideas, I am still puzzled by the magical images (astrological in nature.) You mention in the introduction that using them for everyday memoria may be dangerous, and Bruno may have intended them differently—perhaps as notae, to meditate on, trace and improve one’s memory magically? The book doesn’t expound on this. Could you suggest some techniques or resources for doing this safely?

Sincerely,

MsKrieger

replies.title: Re: Giordano Bruno’s magical images
replies.content: I'm by no means sure I know exactly what Bruno had in mind with the images he took from astrological magic! The ones I chose to be illustrated in the book have benevolent effects, and if you take a few moments to contemplate them from time to time the blessings they bring will have an easier time manifesting in your life -- but beyond that, a great deal of further research and experimentation will be needed to figure out how best to use the images generally.

replies.title: Re: Giordano Bruno’s magical images
replies.content: Thank you for your reply. I had noticed that the illustrations had a definite flavor of benevolence compared to rest of the images described, but I was hesitant to play with fire. The system of Lullian geometry using the Tarot is enough to occupy me for quite a while!

comments.title: Children
comments.content: I know you have touched on this before, but being new to this, I was hoping you could clarify. You have said that ritual magic shouldn't be done when you have young children, which seems like sound advice to me. Does that also apply to practices like the sphere of protection or the AODA grove ritual? It seems like these would be alright, but just want to make sure.

replies.title: Re: Children
replies.content: Those are fine. You can also practice seasonal rituals, and natural magic (the sort that uses herbs, stones, etc.) around young children in perfect safety. It's when you get into serious ceremonial magic -- summoning spirits, consecrating working tools and talismans, and so on -- that you're calling on forces that may not be safe to have around the very young.

replies.title: Re: Children
replies.content: Do you mean don't do the ceremonial magic in front of children or in the same house as children? Or would it even be potentially dangerous to do the ceremonial magic outside of the house and keep it separate from children?

replies.title: Re: Children
replies.content: Don't do it in the same house while they are around. Once they're of age to go to preschool or school, you can most magical practices in the house when they're gone with no ill effect -- though that's partly a function of age.

comments.title: Grove Stones
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

I have a set of Grove Stones that I would like to enchant on the Druid Holy Days. Can I use my already enchanted set of stones, re-enchanting each of them on the appropriate Holy days, or should I take a new set of stones?

Also, can I start this process with the southeastern stone this coming Belteinne (then following south, southwestern, etc) or would I do well to wait until Samhuinn to begin (and if so, why)?

Thank you for your time, and for hosting magic mondays!
- Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Grove Stones
replies.content: You can certainly re-enchant them -- repeated enchantment is worth doing, as it produces stronger results -- and you can start at any point in the cycle.

comments.title: Alternatives to magic
comments.content:
Mr. Greer,

I know you don't recommend the practice magic to people who take psychiatric medication. On the other hand, I know you are not a fan of people trying to get rid of their desires. With these two viewpoints of yours in mind, what's a person who takes psychiatric meds, yet desires to practice magic, to do?

I tried asking myself what it is that draws me to magic, and why I keep coming back to it after all these years. I came up with three things which draw me to magic:

- concentration: being able to concentrate on something with intensity and focus gives me a positive feeling of power,
- purification: I would like to unblock my internal energy channels to improve the vitality of my physical body,
- protection: I want to develop a strong aura to be protected from other people's emotions, as well as potential magical attacks.

What would you recommend that I do? Should I start doing everything I can in order to get myself off antidepressants and be able to safely practice magic, or are there other occult practices which can give me these three benefits and would be safer for me to practice?

I know it's a rather large and serious question, so thank you in advance for any response and also for providing this wonderful opportunity.

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to magic
replies.content:
I'd concentrate on getting off the antidepressants. It's a good standard rule among old-fashioned occult schools that a prospective student should deal with any pressing non-magical issues first, before tackling the hard work of magical training.

One thing only you can decide is just how necessary your antidepressants are. I know there are people who really do need them to get through the day -- but there are also physicians who dispense them very, very freely, not least because pharmaceutical companies give kickbacks to doctors who prescribe their drugs. If you can handle your depressed moods without medicine, without danger to yourself or others, tapering off the drugs is probably a good idea all by itself, over and above its usefulness in preparing you for magical training.

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to magic
replies.content:
I really do need them. I have tried getting off them multiple times, but it always ended badly. This last time I started taking them (about 2 years ago), it took some major effort on the part of my therapist to convince me that I need them. I wanted to do it drug-free, but I just couldn't.

With that said, isn't there some kind of light magic which would be safe even for me to do? For example, I really like Denning & Phillips' Tower of Light exercise, because it does not call on any deities, spirits or the like. That could take care of my needs for concentration and protection (as mentioned in the original post). Do I think I could practice at least this?

Also, I'm huge fan of breathing exercises. Isn't there some safe, proven breathing practice which can start cleaning energetic blocks in the subtle bodies, thereby improving the vitality of the physical body?

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to magic
replies.content: In that case, you probably need to talk to someone who specializes in meditative and mystical approaches to spirituality, and who has some background in working with people who are on antidepressants. Since I don't qualify on either ground, I'll refrain from offering further advice.

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to magic
replies.content:
Okay, I understand, even if I’m not particularly happy about it. Still, such a person might be hard to find, and even harder to evaluate. Could divination help me with these things? I mean, deciding what kinds of practice would be safe for me, or deciding whose teachings can be trusted.

Also, I hereby declare to take full responsibíty for any actions I take as a result of your responses, and I do understand that nothing you write here can be considered medical advice.

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to magic
replies.content: Please see my previous comment.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: For the sake of time efficiency it's tempting to think about doing magic and exercise one after the other. Can you do magic while still sweating and breathing hard? Conversely is it safe to be under a heavy barbell soon after doing magic? Is there a length of time you should wait between magic and exercise or exercise and magic, and should you do something like eat or have a bath between them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Heck of a good question. I don't know that anyone's ever explored this. Me, I'd do the magic first and the exercise later -- calming mind and body for magical practices isn't easy when you're breathing hard and your muscles are shaking from pumping iron -- but your mileage may vary. Since there's no traditional rule forbidding this, I'd encourage you to experiment.

replies.title: both bath and eat
replies.content: I'd recommend the physical exercise first, as we always recommended eating to help ground after the magical exercise, and you don't want to work out on a full stomach.  I would take a bath or shower between the physical and magical exercises, not just as a preparatory cleansing, but to get the sweat off ... you'll have to take my word that it is a little niggling distraction in the back of your mind to be doing ritual with dried (or wet still) sweat and that wonderful aroma.  (Not to mention smears of face-paint/camo plus that three-days-in-the-back-40 funk when you were only given three hours to either do ritual or take a shower.  The battalion chaplain straightened that platoon sergeant out after that particular incident.  For the record, I was neither the first nor the last coven member to come to ritual in that condition.)

comments.title: A few questions
comments.content:
Hi jmg
How can one tell between a magical order and a personality cult?

If i have bad habits or defects of character that i would like to change, would the practice of theurgy help me change them or would would those defects impede me from practicing it?

What do you think most people who get into Magic have in mind about what they can get from it?

Does freemasonry involve magical practice?

I tried googling local magical lodges but i had no results. Do you have any tips on finding one?

Are left hand path magicians edgelords or are there actual practical reasons for choosing the left hand path other than wanting attention or a reason to be mean?

And finally, do you know any texts on astrological theurgy?

Thank you very much!

replies.title: Re: A few questions
replies.content:
Quite a list!

1) The same way you tell whether anything else is a personality cult -- if it centers around a personality, rather than (say) a body of teaching, it's best avoided.

2) Everyone has defects of character. Part of magical training is learning to deal with those, in yourself and others.

3) You'd have to ask them. I haven't done a survey.

4) No.

5) They're not easy to find, because those that make themselves too public get bombarded with inquiries from people who aren't serious about doing the work. Your best bet is to get to work studying and practicing magic on your own, so that you'll know what to look for.

6) What on earth is an "edgelord"?

7) Sure. Can you read ancient Greek?

replies.title: Re: A few questions
replies.content:
Thank you for the answers!
To answer your counter-questions by "edgelords" i mean people who are "edgy", that is, people who like to pretend they are interesting because they do not follow social norms or moral codes. The stereotypical atheist teenager who shares transgressive quotes over a picture of the Joker from Christopher Nolan is an example of someone who fits the label of edgelord.

And no, i can't read ancient greek :(

Another thing: I want to personally apologize for keeping pirated copies of your work. I just deleted all of your books from my drive and will stop trying to acquire more by those means. I feel embarrassed for doing so only after realizing about the means you took to prevent it, and i hope it's one less obstacle between myself and magical practice.

replies.title: Re: A few questions
replies.content:
I'd tend to think of such people as edgeserfs, not edgelords. A lord makes his own decisions, a serf does what other people tell him, and people who choose what to do on the basis of whether it offends someone else is living according to someone else's notions instead of their own.

As for people who are doing left-hand magic, you'd have to ask them. I don't hang out with such people, and so have no clear idea what their motivations are.

If you don't know ancient Greek, your best bet for astrological theurgy is to study Christopher Warnock's books on planetary magic and Gregory Shaw's Theurgy and the Soul. Those will give you a good basis for starting out.

Finally, thank you for your apology! I appreciate that.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Serious question: what are your thoughts on the magical ethics of masturbation? I'm concerned that masturbating imagining people will effect them. I don't want to be etherically rapey, but I do wish to pleasure myself and imagine having sex.

In part, I ask because I've been affected by other people masturbating thinking of me (and it was verified). I also don't want to imagine past lovers.

Your thoughts on this are highly appreciated!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Don't visualize actual people when you masturbate. Your imagination can quite easily come up with appropriate imagery all by itself, if you let it.

comments.title: Ritual space
comments.content:
As I work through your book,Learning Ritual Magic I'm using my home office space for the exercises. Is there a conflict created by performing magical and mundane work in the same space, or is the open/close of each exercise sufficient separation.
I second the above question re exercise and ritual practice, where I would be allowing around 45-60 mins between. I'm guessing exercise resembles eating or sex here.
Thank you for the advice about not mixing Tai chi and GD ritual. Much improved results. Are there compatible or even complimentary martial arts systems you would recommend?
Thanks as always for the excellent help you provide.

replies.title: Re: Ritual space
replies.content:
1) I do my magical practices in the same room I use for writing, so I hope that's okay. ;-)

2) As I noted above, I simply don't know about exercise. Experimentation would be useful here.

3) Western martial arts seem to be perfectly compatible with GD ritual -- many of the original Golden Dawn initiates used boxing and fencing as exercises, for example. I suspect, though this would have to be explored by people who know the arts in question, that most so-called "external" arts (karate, judo, tae kwon do, etc.) should also be fine. It's when you start doing a lot with internal energies that problems pop up.

comments.title: approaching misanthropy
comments.content:
I'm new to occultism and am wondering if a certain practice/ritual could help restore my faith in humanity, or at least keep toxic people out of my life? The cumulative effects of dealing with workplace bullies, toxic supervisors (ridiculing me, taking credit for my work), busybody/malicious neighbors, unsupportive "friends," scoundrel business people, and a lot of general bad luck have worn me down so much.

My coping/defense mechanism is social avoidance and isolation, which I realize is not healthy. Being an empath, being naturally quiet and reserved and probably too self-aware probably doesn't help.

I'm not a malicious person; I try to be kind; I'm not intentionally putting bad energy into the universe. I've tried therapy and didn't find it helpful. I've prayed a lot...and here I am. Any advice? Thank you!

(PS: my mother has faced similar things, and far, far worse--a therapist once started crying after hearing her life story. Maybe someone has hexed us...?)

replies.title: Re: approaching misanthropy
replies.content:
I hear fairly often from people who describe experiences similar to yours. Very consistently they fall into one of two categories.

First, you get the people who've gotten caught by the most toxic of the legacies of institutional Christianity, the notion that weakness is morally good. They've been taught that it's good to be kind, to be quiet, to be meek, to be passive -- that being these things will make them good people -- and then discover the hard way that being these things by and large means that life kicks you repeatedly in the teeth. Baffled and miserable, they keep on trying to follow a misguided definition of what being good means, and they keep on getting kicked in the teeth by life, until they realize that there's something wrong with their basic approach.

That's the first category. The second consists of people who like to wallow in their misery because they've found it's a good way to get attention from others. We've all met them -- the people who love to go on by the hour about their sufferings, and who always have a good reason why there's nothing they can do but keep on suffering. They have what they want out of life, and there's no point in encouraging them to change.

I'm going to assume, though, that you belong to the first category. In that case, yes, magical training can help you quite a bit. It can teach you to develop a strong will, to choose what you want out of life and make that happen, and to find the balance between mercy and severity, kindness and harshness, that will attract the kind of people you want in your life and chase off the ones you don't. It's going to take hard work, and you'll need to be willing to change a lot of the ways you do things, but it's certainly within reach -- if you choose to reach for it.

replies.title: Re: approaching misanthropy
replies.content:
Thank you so much for your reply. Your assumption is correct, I'm definitely in the first category. I actually attended conservative Christian schools for all of elementary and high school. Being kind, quiet, meek, and passive were things consistently reinforced at school, and culturally at home by my immigrant parents. Needless to say, I've got a lot of baggage...

In terms of starting magical training, where do you recommend I begin? There's a lot out there, and it's a bit overwhelming. All I'm familiar with (by way of a YouTube video) is the LBRP, which I've done once. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: approaching misanthropy
replies.content: Are you comfortable with Judeo-Christian symbolism and traditions? If so, the book I recommend for beginners is my book Learning Ritual Magic, which is a basic study course for the complete novice. If not, you might want to consider my book The Druid Magic Handbook, which also starts with the basics and goes from there. Both have been used by a lot of beginners with reliably good results.

comments.title: 12 000 year old terrestrial catastrophe?
comments.content:
Ave Archdruid!

Today I read one of your ADR essays from April 2015, the one from the reporter who missed all the signs, about Atlantis not going to sink, no way, not ever. The date you used, 9-thousand-and-something BC, concurs strongly with the date of a postulated catastrophe on Earth detailed in the book "Watermark", by Joseph Christy-Vitale.

I wonder if you are failiar with that work, or its thesis, that Earth's magnetic field, land formations and climate were altered dramatically by an encounter with cosmic debris from a supernova at that time? - it's the only instance of such claims I've encountered with any shred of accompanying evidence. As one might expect, it seems to have been left out in the cold by the established disciplines whose material it dared to reinterpret.

And I am curious as to the provenance of the date you used in your story, and whether you could suggest any other related material or similar topics.

Thanks and regards
kallianeira.

replies.title: Re: 12 000 year old terrestrial catastrophe?
replies.content: 9600 BCE is the date Plato gives for the sinking of Atlantis, and it's also the end of the Younger Dryas cold period and the beginning of catastrophic glacial melting at the end of the last ice age. A lot of alternative theorists who are interested in catastrophes in recent prehistory have latched onto that date for good reason. I haven't read the specific book you mention -- the last round of reading in that end of the alternative-realities literature I did was in the course of researching my book on Atlantis, somewhat unoriginally titled Atlantis. There's a huge amount of literature out there on such topics, some better documented than others; it's always seemed to me, though, that the basic fact of the end of the last ice age -- which caused cataclysmic floods in low-lying areas around the globe, backed up with huge tsunamis caused by several known geological factors, and drastic changes in climate, is more than enough to account for the evidence without dragging in supernovae. Still, since I haven't read Christy-Vitale, I'll reserve judgment until I've read his book.

comments.title: Ancestor spirits
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. I have a question about ancestor spirits and the safest way to interact with them.

First, some background:
I was adopted at birth. I found my birth mother when I was in my mid-20's, but it took a lot longer to find my father's side. In fact, I only found them recently. I haven't contacted any living people, and I'm not sure I want to. I don't want to disrupt anyone's lives, just to know more about where I came from.

But as I was looking at some old photographs of my grandparents' generation (found on-line, on a genealogy site), I had a strong desire to at least honor them in some way and perhaps to have some kind of contact with them. I know there are many ancestor traditions all over the world, but what I was wondering is whether there are any major warnings or safeguards you would give to someone interested in that.

I'm not practicing ritual magic currently, but I do have a regular prayer practice and I'm familiar with basic natural magic.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor spirits
replies.content: I don't do ancestor work, and am not very well informed about it, so can't advise you here. You'd probably be best off finding someone who's experienced in that end of magic.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor spirits
replies.content: Thanks very much for your reply. I'll look around and see what I can find.

As an amusing aside, your comment on the occult number line reminded me of a passage in the second OZ book: The Land of OZ, where the instructions on a bit of magic powder say to to count to 17 by 2s, and Tip begins with 1/2.

comments.title: Alternative to banishing/mirroring back?
comments.content:
Dear John Michael,

I would like your thoughts on the following idea for dealing with negativity that might come one's way. I am a highly sensitive/intuitive person, deeply empathic, and it has been a journey to be healthy with that capacity! And to not play "psychic garbage collector." I'm in a pretty good place now, but when I think of "banishing" and "mirroring back" negativity--whether simply emotions, ill intent, or even concerted black magic type messing about--this doesn't feel so great. It's just sending nasty back, even if it didn't originate with me. And the negative forces remain in the total ecology of our shared environment.

One healer offered that she instead surrounds herself with a kind of cushion of divine goodness/benevolence, and intends that anything which comes at her be met by this benevolent divine. The person can have whatever experience they want or need to have (good/bad/neutral), and may even think they're having some experience with her, but whatever--just another variant on projection. In that way, there is at least an attempt to offer healing for the unwell, but it doesn't have to be actively processed/dealt with by the healer herself. It's worked quite well for her, so far.

I am intrigued. I think it takes cultivating strong relations with source/god/divine/whatever the label first of all, and having a clear sense of one's self in relation to that divinity. But do you have any thoughts on this as a tactic?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Alternative to banishing/mirroring back?
replies.content: Interesting. I'd say give it a try and see how well it works for you.

replies.title: Re: Alternative to banishing/mirroring back?
replies.content: Psychic Protection by William Bloom addresses some of these questions.  Great book that I’ve found really helpful.

comments.title: Sabian symbols
comments.content:
What is your opinion of the Sabian symbols? Do you use them, and if so how?

What I’ve seen online so far suggests that the most familiar way is to observe the sun’s position at dawn on a given day. Assuming this is valid, how would one apply it?

Is it fruitful to pay attention to the passage of the Sun through the Sabian symbols in relation to the features of a horoscope? Can they play a role in the interpretation of a natal (or horary or mundane or other) chart, or are they better taken as a system unto themselves?

“We are Sabians of Harran” is my favorite historical dodge.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Sabian symbols
replies.content: I haven't looked into them. I've been studying astrology systematically for only about eight years now, and there are still quite a few branches of it that I haven't explored.

replies.title: Re: Sabian symbols
replies.content:
Thanks for your response. That being the case, I may try a little research project. I’ll check out which Sabian symbol is rising for a given day, and see how that pans out over time.

Here’s my methodology: I’ll use the chart software available on Astrodienst, and the Sabian symbols as listed on this site and page:

https://cafeastrology.com/sabiansymbols_degreemeanings.html

The moment of the Sun’s rising will be when the mid-point of the Sun coincides with the Ascendant/horizon, as nearly as Astrodienst will allow (viz., to the nearest clock minute). This is in contradistinction to the appearance of the Sun’s limb over the Horizon, which I cannot readily observe (my weather app puts it about five minutes earlier).

Today’s Sabian symbol as seen near San Francisco is 4 degrees Taurus 19 minutes. It is:

A Widow At An Open Grave

Maybe the gods are trying to cheer me up?

Going with the weather app’s time for dawn it would be in the third degree of Taurus:

Natural Steps Lead To A Lawn Of Clover In Bloom

But this puts the Sun a degree or two below the horizon. I don’t know whether it counts or not.

No planets or other features show up from my natal chart for about a month. It should be interesting.

Kevin

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thank you for the Magic Monday!

Do you have any idea on how our society has been able to get so disconnected from reality? There are plenty of times where I'm left shaking my head at how absurd something that almost everyone else takes for granted is, and it seems like their under a spell of some sort.

Do you know of a way to get at least some people to notice the real world?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This kind of thing happens reliably at certain phases in the historical cycle. What Giambattista Vico called "the barbarism of reflection," the state when abstractions pile up so fast and thick that they make it very hard to see the concrete realities of existence.

Getting them to notice -- well, that doesn't tend to work very well. All you can do is work on seeing things clearly yourself, and helping those who want to be helped.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I wonder if, rather than the problem being abstractions, it’s that many find the reality of 21st-century America unbearable.  If you’re lucky, you’re chained to a shitty job so you can see a doctor if you need to; businesses devote great effort to making their products and services worse;  your vote is meaningless as the political system has been entirely corrupted; you have no privacy; you’re surrounded by ugliness.  No wonder a lot of people can’t face all that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No doubt that's also a part of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: And also, discussion is taboo.  Whenever someone tries to address a national problem, the sheep start bleating “This is The Greatest Country In The World!”  How can you solve problems you can’t discuss?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've been wondering for a while now what's behind that. This country wasn't always terrified of change, or of facing its problems...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think the egregor went bad, starting in the ‘60’s and early ‘70’s as corruption began to be expected.  It was always around, but there had been at least a pro forma effort to keep it within bounds.  President Carter’s term was the last manifestation of probity.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: So it's the old issue of the tainted sphere. Yes, I could see that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Do you know of anything individual citizens can do to help clean up the egregor?

Also, would anyone else like to BE a citizen instead of a “consumer”? I can’t remember the last time a news story called Us The People anything other than “consumers.”

comments.title: Middle pillar

replies.title: Re: Middle pillar

comments.title: Eyes Wide Shut
comments.content:
I have been practicing daily discursive meditation along with fourfold breath exercises for about five months now. I find it easier to concentrate when I do so with my eyes closed. I have experimented with open-eyes meditation and I tend to go off topic more easily.

This might be partly because I sometimes get so immersed in the meditation theme that my eyes go out of focus, or my field of vision begins to get distorted. I had vision problems as a child, so this isn't a great feeling for me.

Am I missing an important part of the practice if I am mostly meditating with eyes closed? I don't want to be cheating myself by taking the easy road...

replies.title: Re: Eyes Wide Shut
replies.content: Either way is fine. I know good practitioners who do it either way -- and there are no points gained by making the work harder than it has to be.

comments.title: theurgy
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Are the works of William Walker Atkinson Theurgy? Are his writings on will power, concentration, and other mental works a good place to start magical training?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content:
Atkinson! Now there's a grand old name. His work is part of the occult wing of the old New Thought movement, the New Age of the late 19th century, and was hugely influential in early 20th century American occultism. I've worked with his books extensively, and find them worth close study and practice; if you feel attracted to his approach, by all means go to work with his books -- you'll learn a great deal from them.

The term "theurgy," though, traditionally means magical practices that focus on interaction with the divine, and Atkinson wasn't deeply into that.

replies.title: "coincidence"
replies.content:
This is what our nonmagical acquaintances will superstitiously call a coincidence - I also found several old Atkinson books on GoogleBooks this week, and thought about asking your opinion of him, then decided I want to read a couple for myself first. I had been searching for old yoga books, and found him that way, and GoogleBooks put several other titles by him and his various pseudonyms in their "related books" part under the description.

If at least two of us found him this past week, I suspect there is something he wrote that is wanting to come back out into the public sphere.

replies.title: Re: "coincidence"
replies.content: I hope so. Some of the things he taught -- especially his system of emotional alchemy, which teaches the student to guide the swings of emotion in productive ways, rather than splashing them all anyhow onto the world -- could be very helpful just now.

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content:
I would like to echo our host's delighted reaction at hearing William Walker Atkinson mentioned. He is one of the very best writers in the occult wing of the New Thought movement. My favorite among his many books is _Mind Power: The Secret of Mental Magic_ (1912), which is a light revision of two of his earlier books in combination, namely, _The Secret of Mental Magic_ and _Mental Fascination_ (both 1907). Also, he is almost certainly all three of the "Three Initiates" who wrote _The Kybalion_ (1908).

Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content:
As you know, Robert, but some others may not, he's also Theron Q. Dumont, the author of a flurry of books on mind power; Yogi Ramacharaka, the author of influential early books on yoga and physical culture; and Magus Incognito, the author of Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians -- a book that greatly influenced Dion Fortune by way of her teacher Dr. Theodore Moriarty, who quoted it at great length in his book The Seven Aphorisms of Creation.

The early 20th century Druid Revival correspondence course of which I inherited fragments, and filled out with other material and my own work to create The Dolmen Arch correspondence course, also drew on Atkinson; in my reconstruction, I borrowed heavily from The Arcane Lessons, among other Atkinson works.

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content: Didn't Atkinson write a book on logic as well?

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content: Quite possibly, though I'm not familiar with it. Something else that would be rather useful just now!

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content:
From an "Other books by this author" list in 1912 Mind Power is it The Art of Logical Thinking, or the Laws of Reasoning?

Yet another interesting title to add to the rapidly-growing list.

comments.title: Not Magic Related
comments.content:
But this is a very interesting article. I got money that says you will go OOHHH.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1804.03748.pdf

replies.title: Re: Not Magic Related
replies.content: Yes, I saw that! "Oooh" isn't a bad rendition of my reaction, either, though a sigh of relief was also involved. If scientists are finally starting to grapple with the fragility of industrial civilizations, the odds that something constructive will be done about the brittleness of this one go up substantially.

replies.title: Re: Not Magic Related
replies.content: The most interesting part of this paper, to me, was the point that a civilization that charges headfirst off the cliff and destroys itself would leave a boundary layer that is distinctive but thin so hard to find, whereas a technological species that pulls its head out of its nether regions and becomes sustainable enough to thrive for a longer time leaves less obvious traces of itself - so either way, the chance of finding good evidence of its existence after tens of millions of years would be very low.  Thus I can continue to believe in the Great Old Ones.  ;-)  - Dewey

comments.title: Definitions
comments.content:
Thank you so much for these Magic Monday discussions. I always read, even when I do not write, and I find them informative and thought-provoking.

The thought that comes to mind this week is where you draw the line between magic / magical practice, religion / religious practice, and ritual practice. It seems that ritual could be magic or could be religion (if not both). And I have the impression (coming as I do from a long background in Tibetan Buddhist practices) that some religious practices definitely have a magical aspect to them, and perhaps vice versa.

Thank you for your thoughts on this. I'm feeling the need for clarification as I proceed along my current path,
Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Definitions
replies.content: It's not so much a line as a contested borderland. On the one hand you have practices such as devotional prayer and the formal reading of scriptures, which are pretty definitely religious. On the other you have practices such as consecrating talismans and invoking spirits, which are pretty definitely magical. Then you have various shades of overlap -- magical workings that invoke gods and goddesses, religious workings that seek practical goals, and so on. Both religion and magic use ritual as a basic tool, and both swipe useful ritual gimmicks from each other, so it's hard to be clear. It's especially hard with Tibetan Buddhism or Hermeticism, both of which tend to spend a lot of time within the contested zone!
